Empires & Puzzles Greel Review: The 3-Star Finisher You Didn't Know You Needed

Greel is a 3-star Dark hero from the Abyss Hunter family. With a Very Slow speed but potential for massive damage and mana gain, he's a niche specialist. Is he worth the tiles, or should he stay in the abyss?
Greel: The Tiny Reaper With Big Dreams (and Slow Mana)
Greel: The Chainsaw-Wielding Goblin That Wants To Be Treevil When He Grows Up
, is a Rare (3-star) Dark hero from the
(Monster Hunter) family. Released in 2023 during the Covenant Summon era, he burst onto the scene with a mechanic that screams "Finisher." While his

mana speed might make seasoned players recoil in horror, Greel serves a very specific purpose: cleaning up the mess his teammates left behind. He’s essentially a specialized janitor for your enemy's health bars.

Greel's design is classic goblin chaos. He sports a horned helmet that looks slightly too big, a makeshift weapon that appears to be a jawbone tied to a stick, and an expression that says, "I'm going to bite your ankles." The purple and green color palette fits perfectly with the Abyss Hunter theme, giving him a toxic, swamp-dweller vibe. It’s a fun, gritty look that perfectly communicates his role as a scrappy, underhanded fighter.
When compared to the modern crop of 3-star heroes, Greel is starting to show his age.
This is significantly lower than modern 3-star attackers like
(533). For a hero whose sole job is dealing damage, this is a drawback.
These numbers are dangerously low, especially for a
hero who needs to survive long enough to fire. Modern tanks like
(908 HP) eclipse him completely in bulk.
His stats are outdated. He relies heavily on his special skill multiplier and passives to remain relevant.
"Stubby Reap" is a high-risk, high-reward skill. At first glance, 210% damage to all enemies at Very Slow speed is terrible. However, the magic lies in the conditional
it. If you fire him when the enemy team is softened up, he hits like a truck.
The +20% mana per defeated enemy is the cherry on top. If he wipes 3 enemies, he gains 60% mana instantly, putting him well on his way to firing again.
You have to get him to fire. At Very Slow speed with low defenses, he often dies before he can lift his weapon.

This rivals the total output of Average speed hit-all heroes, but requires setup.

Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
The Abyss Hunter family brings some interesting utility to the table.

bonus boosts attack, defense, or health based on the number of unique family members. While useful, you are unlikely to run a full team of Abyss Hunters in a 3-star tournament, so consider this a minor stat boost rather than a game-changer.
This is excellent utility. Transferring a status ailment (like Defense Down or Poison) to an enemy
hitting them is fantastic. If he’s clean, he heals 12%, which helps his survivability slightly.
The Hunter's Mark adds 150 damage over 3 turns (50 per turn). It’s not huge, but in 3-star tournaments, every bit of chip damage counts.

gives him a mana generation buff at the start of battle. For a Very Slow hero, this is arguably the best possible power (outside of straight mana) as it helps him charge slightly faster in those critical opening turns.
Upon release, Greel was immediately identified as a "Rush Attack specialist." He was often paired with
to devastate enemy teams in Very Fast tournaments.
Greel has suffered from stat creep. New heroes hit harder and faster. His reliance on the enemy being below 50% HP is a restrictive condition in a meta where healing and overhealing are rampant.
He is no longer a general-purpose hero. He lives and dies in
. Outside of that mode, he is too slow and too fragile to be competitive against modern 3-stars.
As of this review, Greel does not have a costume.
Greel is a Barbarian. His class talent (Bleed) fits his aggressive nature, but his primary need is
to ensure he fires his skill, or
Maximizes the 315% multiplier. Ensures that if he fires, enemies die.
He remains extremely squishy and might die to a few slashes or a sniper hit before charging.
Gives him the bulk needed to survive 13.5 tiles of play. Makes him more viable as a tank or flank in Rush.
His kill potential drops slightly, risking leaving enemies with 1 HP.
is recommended. If you are bringing Greel, you are gambling on him firing once to end the game. You want that one hit to be decisive. If you need a tank, use a tank; use Greel to kill.
Can recharge quickly if he secures kills.
In Very Fast modes, his speed penalty vanishes, making him a top-tier threat.
Very Slow is painful in normal play.
Low defense and health stats by modern standards.
If enemies are above 50% HP, his damage is underwhelming (210%).
Needs teammates to soften targets first.
This is his home. When set to Very Fast speed, he becomes a monster.
Good for clearing waves of mobs if timed correctly to get the mana refund.
The lack of healing means enemies stay under 50% HP more often, triggering his bonus damage.
Greel is a combo piece. Never bring him alone.

Treevil fires first (Very Fast in Rush), applying Defense Down to everyone. Greel follows up, hitting the def-downed enemies. If they survive Treevil, they are likely under 50% HP, triggering Greel's massive hit.
Fast hitters like Maeve and Noril drop enemy health quickly. Greel sits in the wing, waiting to fire his "Stubby Reap" once the enemies are in the kill range.
(e.g., Pixie, Hansel) shut him down completely.
(e.g., Faiez) keep enemies above 50% HP, nullifying his damage bonus.
generally don't help him directly. He prefers teammates who deal direct damage or Defense Down.
If you pull Greel early, hold onto him, but
. Level your Fast and Average speed 3-stars first (like Bane, Tyrum, or Valen). Greel is a luxury tool for tournaments, not a campaign carry.

You likely have him sitting at 1/1 or maxed for Rush. He is not worth a Limit Break 2 (Alpha Aether) unless you are extremely competitive in 3-star Rush tournaments. The stats gained from LB2 help, but his utility is capped by his speed mechanic. Save your Alpha Aethers for heroes with more versatility.
The community reaction to Greel has always been mixed, centering on his synergy with Treevil.
"Treevil's new sidekick? Yup. Pain and suffering."
- Many players immediately saw the potential of pairing him with the Rush Attack king.
"If Greel’s special skill kills all 5 opponents, the mana bar would be instantly filled?"
- Yes, this mechanic was a source of great excitement (and fear) upon release.
"I’d hate to be that one Fighter class hero that gets resurrected and has to face the Stubby Reap all by their lonesome…again."
- A hilarious but accurate observation of his ability to loop his special.

Greel is the definition of a "One-Trick Pony," but it's a pretty cool trick. He isn't going to win you every raid, and he's terrible for farming season stages. But when the stars align in a Rush Tournament and he wipes an entire team off the board in one swing, you'll be glad you leveled the little guy.
Keep him for Rush Attack, bench him for everything else.
